coredata.dc:title: Chitosan and carboxymethyl cellulose-multilayered magnetic fluorescent systems for reversible protein immobilization
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lei
Wang, Feijun
Shao, Ziqiang
Liu, Jianxin
Zhang, Qiaoling
Jiao, Weizhou
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles (MFNPs)-based protein immobilization systems were successfully synthesized via a layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly approach in an impinging stream-rotating packed bed, which adopted chitosan containing carbon dots (CDs)@Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) as a precursor, chitosan (CS) or carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) involving CDs as shells. To reveal the relationship between structure and efficiency of systems, the effect of self-assembly mode and layer number\was investigated to provide us insight into how to improve the design of MFNPs-based supports. Finally, an MFNPs-based protein immobilization system with excellent fluorescence and magnetic response, expanded specific surface area, and enhanced immobilization and release performance were obtained. Immobilization mechanism and kinetic studies were also conducted to reveal the rate-controlling step and the template affinity. Taken together, this study provides an effective strategy to synthesize bifunctional MFNPs and to adjust the performance, which aims for facilitating new possibilities for biomass-based nanomaterials used for protein immobilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Carbon dots
Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Layer-by-layer self-assembly
Bovine serum albumin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Technological Innovation Project of Beijing Institute of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Graduate Technological Innovation Project of Beijing Institute of Technology (BIT) (grant number 2017CX10023 ). We thank the Instrumental Analysis Center of Beijing Institute of Technology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of biomass-multilayered magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles (MFNPs) via high-gravity strategies. (b) Schematic representation of layer-by-layer self-assembly fabrication. (c) The as-prepared MFNPs can be well-dispersed in water, exhibit strong fluorescent emission, and be collected in an external magnetic field. (d) The CDs or CMC molecular chains can anchor on CS molecular chains via electrostatic attraction to form stable structures.

TEM image of (a) pristine CDs, (b) CCF, (d) A-MFNPs-3, (e) B-MFNPs-3 and (f) B-MFNPs-5. Inset of (b) is HRTEM image of FCCs. (c) represent the homogeneous distribution of C (red), Fe (yellow), N (blue) and O (green) of CCF. Inset of (f) shows biomass construct on the surface of MFNPs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Characterization of different MFNPs. (a) XPS survey spectra of the MFNPs samples. (b − e) Fe 2p, C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum of B-MFNPs-5. (f) FT-IR spectra of samples.

(a) XRD patterns of the samples. (b) Hydrodynamic size distributions of different samples measured using dynamic light scattering. (c) Changes of zeta potential during self-assembly. (d) Effect of pH on zeta-potentials of the samples. (e) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution of different MFNPs samples. (f) TGA curve of different MFNPs samples.

(a) Magnetization curve of different MFNPs samples. (b) Variation of the maximum emission wavelength of different MFNPs with different excitation wavelength, the inset shows the digital photos of CCF dispersion under different excitation wavelength. (c) MTT assay of HeLa and HeLung cell viability after incubation with the B-MFNPs-3 NPs at different concentrations for 48 h at 37 °C.

(a) Effect of contact time on equilibrium immobilization capacity of MFNPs. (b) Linear fitting results of the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. (c) Linear fitting results of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. (d) Linear fitting results of the intraparticle-diffusion model.
Equilibrium data for BSA immobilization onto different MFNPs.
Immobilization capacity of BSA onto the different supports.

row.entry: Core-sheath CTA-HAp/CTA nanofibers (Lan, Shao, Wang, & Gu, 2015)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Kinetic parameters of BSA immobilization onto the MFNPs.
Kinetic and thermodynamic parameters of BSA immobilization onto the MFNPs.

head.ce:title: Chitosan and carboxymethyl cellulose-multilayered magnetic fluorescent systems for reversible protein immobilization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Engineering Research Centre of Cellulose and Its Derivatives, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Engineering Research Centre of Cellulose and Its Derivatives
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Higee-Oriented Chemical Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Higee-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles (MFNPs)-based protein immobilization systems were successfully synthesized via a layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly approach in an impinging stream-rotating packed bed, which adopted chitosan containing carbon dots (CDs)@Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) as a precursor, chitosan (CS) or carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) involving CDs as shells. To reveal the relationship between structure and efficiency of systems, the effect of self-assembly mode and layer number\was investigated to provide us insight into how to improve the design of MFNPs-based supports. Finally, an MFNPs-based protein immobilization system with excellent fluorescence and magnetic response, expanded specific surface area, and enhanced immobilization and release performance were obtained. Immobilization mechanism and kinetic studies were also conducted to reveal the rate-controlling step and the template affinity. Taken together, this study provides an effective strategy to synthesize bifunctional MFNPs and to adjust the performance, which aims for facilitating new possibilities for biomass-based nanomaterials used for protein immobilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan/Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs), as a typical organic-inorganic composites (Chiang et al., 2018), are gaining increasing interest for their surface activity, biocompatibility, magnetic drivability, physicochemical robustness and low cost, has been applied to expanded field such as heavy metal adsorption (Fan, Zhou, Jiao, Qi, & Liu, 2017), lipase immobilization (Wu, Wang, Luo, & Dai, 2009), glucose biosensor (Yang, Ren, Tang, & Zhang, 2009), hyperthermic treatment (Mai et al., 2012), electrochemistry (Wang et al., 2013), tissue engineering (Kumari & Singh, 2012), catalyst (Qiu, Ma, & Hu, 2014) and so on. Currently, great research progress has been achieved in chitosan/Fe3O4 design for investigating the interaction of proteins with polyelectrolyte layers (Hubbell & Chilkoti, 2012; Kakkar, Traverso, Farokhzad, Weissleder, & Langer, 2017). Protein separation is a crucial subject in many sciences including biomaterials, biosensor biomedical and biotechnology (Demir, Şenel, & Baykal, 2014; Qian et al., 2017). It has been shown that proteins can be easily immobilized in CS/Fe3O4 NPs and release from polyelectrolyte shell by changing the environmental conditions (Li, Wang, & Shao, 2018). However, the rigid surface property, respond and pore structure of magnetic chitosan NPs have failed to meet the immobilization needs of proteins with various characteristics (Li & Mooney, 2016), as well as apply in fluorescence imaging. Owing to their rapid magnetic and photoluminescence (PL) responses, polyelectrolyte-multilayered and fluorescent-labeled chitosan/Fe3O4 NPs evoked huge research attention as candidates for targeted immobilization and release of encapsulated compounds (Lu et al., 2017). These NPs have been highly praised for achieving directly magnetically guidance and recovery under fluorescence or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Liu et al., 2013; Shen, Tang, Zhang, Chen, & Zhang, 2012; Zamora-Mora et al., 2017). In addition, because of their conventional synthetic method are stepwise deposition and allowing for adopting of various polyelectrolyte modified with different reactive chemical groups, these systems possess high immobilization capacity and adjustable surface properties (Ji et al., 2017). Currently, different novel strategies have also been explored to realize the above-mentioned NPs, typically, a layer-by-layer (LbL) self-assembly technology is applied to build the suitable multi-core-shell architecture (Zhang et al., 2017). However, these methods are often plagued by some critical issues (Seong, Yun, & Park, 2018; Tan et al., 2017). First, the binding capacity of the polyelectrolyte to the magnetic core is relatively weak, thus the thickness of the layer of electrolytes is usually uncontrollable as time process (Anirudhan, Nair, & Bino, 2017). Second, the location of the fluorescent labeling material is difficult to achieve a balance between reducing migration and avoiding excessive bonding to polyelectrolyte, thus inhibiting agglomeration and realize stable luminescence. Third, the biotoxicity of the fluorescent labeling material and polyelectrolyte increase the risk while applying in the biomedical area, because of these systems should be designed to possess active targeting, and reduced side effects (Esmaeili & Haseli, 2017; Gayathri et al., 2017).

According to these views, how to realize the controllability of the thickness and performance of the polyelectrolytes layer are still long-standing challenges. As a method to simultaneously achieve process intensification (PI) and co-precipitation, the high-gravity (HG) technology can realize the scalable and continuous production of NPs with nanoscale and narrow size dispersion (Fan, Zhou, Qi, & Liu, 2016; Sun et al., 2017). PI has drawn tremendous attention because of its ability to significantly reduce operating cost and space requirement while enhancing process yield/selectivity and improving the inherent safety. In 1983s, Ramshaw designed the rotating packed bed (RPB) (Ramshaw, 1983), as a novel gas-liquid reactor, it that utilizes centrifugal field caused by high-speed rotation of packing to intensify mixing and mass transfer. Recently, the application of RPB has been expanded to many systems such as absorption, distillation, stripping, chemical reaction, and ozone oxidation. In particular, Chen et al. (Jiang et al., 2015) have applied of RPB in liquid-liquid precipitation processes to product nanomaterials such as calcium carbonate, aluminum hydroxide, barium carbonate, and Nano drugs. Liu et al. (Jiao, Liu, & Qi, 2012; Jiao, Qin, et al., 2017) developed a novel reactor coupled micro-impact reactor and RPB for further intensifying liquid-liquid mixing, named Impinging Stream-Rotating Packed Bed (IS-RPB), which has been applied to liquid membrane separation, emulsion, extraction, and chemical reaction. The IS-RPB, which can strongly enhance the mass-transfer and micro-mixing efficiency, is very appropriate to establish super-saturation and uniform nucleation (Jiao, Luo, He, & Liu, 2017). Our previous work also has successfully produced nano-sized CS/CDs@Fe3O4 in IS-RPB and applied for anticancer drug delivery (Li, Wang et al., 2018). These NPs are excellent precursors to synthesize multishell nanospheres compared to pristine nanomagnetic cores. Based on this, an LbL self-assembly approach in IS-RPB can be adopted to overcome the potential issues of secondary agglomeration, low fluorescence intensity, rigid surface properties and unsatisfactory Pore structure (Qi et al., 2011). Moreover, to overcome potential biological toxicity, a number of biomass derivatives have been considered as polyelectrolyte shells and fluorescent labeling materials (Li, Jia et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2017). As green materials with low toxicity, acacia gum (Singh, Sharma, & Dhiman, 2017), carrageenan (Sathuvan et al., 2017), guar gum (Kono, Otaka, & Ozaki, 2014; Seeli & Prabaharan, 2017), starch (El-Hag Ali & AlArifi, 2009), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) (Assaad, Wang, Zhu, & Mateescu, 2011; Rasoulzadeh & Namazi, 2017), chitosan (CS) (Kong et al., 2017; Racine, Costa, Bayma-Pecit, Texier, & Auzely-Velty, 2017) and cellulose-derived carbon dots (CDs) (Shen et al., 2016) have gained extremely attracting in different biomedical applications. In particular, cellulose-derived CDs can overcome the shortcomings of traditional fluorescent labeling materials such as organic dyes, rare earth luminescent materials and semiconductor QDs, because of their excellent optical properties of wavelength dependence, superior biocompatibility, salient stability, and easy-to-function surface.

In this paper, to prepare the immobilization systems with enhanced protein encapsulation capacity, low biological toxicity, and reduced cost, a facile preparation method based on HG and LbL self-assembly approaches have been established, which can controllably and orderly combine of Fe3O4, biomass-based polyelectrolytes and fluorescent marks (Fig. 1 a). This method is expected to put forward a new way for controllable construction of multifunctional nanomaterials with controllable surface properties and PL intensity. Moreover, the effect of self-assembly mode (the location of fluorescent materials) and the layer number of polymer electrolyte were investigated to reveal the relationship between structure, properties and immobilization efficiency. The bovine serum albumin (BSA) is chosen as a template protein because it can be used to bind, transport, and load the fatty acids, porphyrins, bilirubin, and cholesterol (Qian et al., 2017). Therefore, we believed that the method to controllably construct non-toxic nanostructure would be provided great superiority in protein immobilization systems, which would also show high potential in situ testing magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), fluorescence imaging, magnetic fluid hyperthermia treatment, and relative research.

The cotton powder and urea were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis tubing with a diameter of 28 mm and a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1 kDa was bought from Biotopped (Viskase, US). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were bought from Guangfu Regent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC, DS = 1.05) was synthesized via a slurry process in a solution of isopropyl alcohol in our laboratory. Chitosan (CS) with an average molecular weight of 161 kDa and a deacetylation degree of 90% was provided by National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC (Darmstadt, Germany). All chemicals were used without further purification. All working solutions and stocks were prepared in deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm, obtained via a GWA-UN to Pure & Ultrapure water purification system (Purkinje General).

The CDs were prepared using a green and eco-friendly hydrothermal method via the reaction between cotton power and urea (Shen et al., 2016). 0.5 g cotton powder and 0.35 g urea were uniformly dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic. The mixed solution is transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor tank and heated to 180 °C. After reaction for 72 h and naturally cooled to room temperature. After centrifuged, a dialysis tubing (MWCO = 1 kDa) was used to remove residual small molecules in the supernatant. The CS/CDs@Fe3O4 (CCF) NPs were in situ prepared through one-step high-gravity reactive precipitation of FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O by NaOH solution in the presence of CDs and CS in the IS-RPB under nitrogen atmosphere protection (More details about the IS-PRB equipment and preparation procedure can be seen in Supplemental Files) (Li, Wang et al., 2018). In general, CS is provided by a positively charged surface, whereas CDs and CMC possess negative charge on their surface, two different self-assembly paths were designed to fabricate multilayer MFNPs from CCF NPs via anionic-cationic electrostatic attractions in IS-RPB (Fig. 1b). (i) CMC standard solution contained 5 wt% CDs and CCF NPs precursor suspension were simultaneously and continuously pumped into different liquid distributors of the IS-RPB at the same liquid flow rate of 60 L h−1, after reaction and washing, the as-obtained NPs further collided with CS standard solution in a constant liquid flow rate, the two steps were repeated to obtained multilayered MFNPs referred to (CMC/CDs-CS)n@CCF (A-MFNPs-(2n+1)). (ii) In IS-RPB, CMC standard solution and CCF NPs precursor suspension were strongly mixed to obtain CMC-coated CCF NPs, then the purified NPs was impacted with CS standard solution contained 5 wt% CDs with a stable fluorescence emission (The changes in PL intensity within three days are shown in Fig. S2), the process was repeated to obtain polyelectrolyte-multilayered MFNPs referred to (CMC-CS/CDs)n@CCF (B-MFNPs-(2n+1)). The CS standard solution was dubbed via dissolving 1 g CS in 1 L acetic acid solution at pH = 5.0, CMC standard solution was prepared using dissolving 1 g CMC in 1 L deionized water. In particular, the pH value of suspension should be adjusted to 5.0 using acetic acid before reacted with CS solution. All MFNPs obtained in each step were washed using deionized water or 1 wt% acetic acid solution three times.

MTT assay was adopted to determine the cytotoxicity of the MFNPs in vitro via human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) and human embryo lung (HeLung) normal cells. In brief, the two cells were incubated with different MFNPs concentrations (10∼00 μg mL−1) with 200 μL culture medium at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After incubated for 48 h (5% CO2, 37 °C), 20 μL of MTT stock solution was added to each well, and the cells were maintained for another 4 h. At last, 0.15 mL of DMSO was applied to completely exchange the medium in each well. The Cell Counting Kit-8 was used to characterize the viability of the cells at a detection wavelength of 490 nm.

The as-prepared MFNPs sample (50 mg) was immersed in 10 mL BSA PBS buffer at stable concentrations and pH 8. The suspensions were shaken at a constant rate (140 rpm) at different temperature (25, 35 and 45 °C). The protein supernatant (0.5 mL) was withdrawn at given time intervals and replaced with the same volume of fresh PBS (37 °C) to maintain a consistent volume. The MFNPs were magnetically separated from the solution to determine the BSA concentration. The protein immobilization efficiency was calculated from the formula as follows: Immobilization Efficiency = W Immobilization protein/W MFNPs×100%. Where W Immobilization protein refers to the weight of immobilized protein, W MFNPs represents the MFNPs weight in feed.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TECNAI G2 F20) together with the energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) mapping were applied to characterize the particle size and morphological features of samples. XRD patterns of the samples were recorded on X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D8 ADVANCE) with Cu-Kα radiation at a voltage of 50 kV. The scanning rate was 4° min−1 and the scanning scope of 2 θ was from 2 to 80° at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were analyzed on an Amicus Budget spectrometer using Al Kα X-ray source (1486.8 eV) operating at 15 kV. The chemical composition was examined by a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer Spectrum Two) in the region of 400-4000 cm−1. The optical absorption region of the samples was recorded with UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, PerkinElmer Lambda 35). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution (Quantachrome NOVA-4000e) was applied to determine the pore structure. The thermal stability of the sample is characterized via thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, NETZS CH STA 409/PC). PL spectra data were recorded from a steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000) at room temperature; the excitation wavelength was chosen at 320–500 nm. The magnetic properties of NPs confirmed by measuring hysteresis loops using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Lakeshore Instrument Plant). The zeta potentials and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were analyzed using a zeta potential analyzer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation).

The morphology of CDs and MFNPs were observed using HRTEM (Fig. 2 ) and SEM (Fig. S3). The as-obtained CDs exhibited a diameter of 3 nm (Fig. 2a), which was uniformly dispersion in water. The MFNPs precursors from IS-RPB showed a quasi-spherical morphology with uniform size distribution, which average particle sizes can be calculated as approximately 8 nm, owing to the process intensification effect, including intensely micro-mixing, homogeneous supersaturating and uniform nucleation (Fan et al., 2017). The extremely short mixing time suppress the excess combination of CDs and CS on the magnetic core, a thin coating layer might be obtained, which is beneficial to increase the number of polyelectrolyte layers and improve the surface properties while maintaining nanosized particle size (Anirudhan, Divya, & Nima, 2016). The local HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 2b) of CCF exhibited complete crystal phase at the center and semitransparent and amorphous structure at the edge. In the crystalline region, the lattice fringes of individual CCF with a d spacing of 0.35 nm can be attributed to the (101) lattice planes of Fe3O4 (Zengin Kurt, Uckaya, & Durmus, 2017) (inset of Fig. 2b). The EDS mapping results of CCF (Fig. 2c) confirmed the uniform distribution of Fe, C, N, and O elements, further demonstrating the CS have been incorporated into the Fe3O4 NPs. To reveal the structure of the MFNPs obtained via different synthetic path, the microscopic morphology of (CS-CMC/CDs)1@CCF (A-MFNPs-3), (CS/CDs-CMC)1@CCF (B-MFNPs-3) and (CS/CDs-CMC)2@CCF (B-MFNPs-5) (Fig. 2d–f) were recorded via TEM. All MFNPs exhibited a core-shell structure with a polymer layer. The thickness of each layer can be estimated as about 2 nm (inset of Fig. 2f). Furthermore, although the onion-like MFNPs were self-assembled using analogous methods, there are certain differences between the two types of MFNPs: the surface of A-MFNPs is more irregular than the B-MFNPs. The possible explanation is that some of the CDs are exposed to the surface of MFNPs, which provided a more active surface.

The surface elemental composition of the MFNPs was investigated by XPS analysis. The spectra of A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 (Fig. 3 a) exhibited almost identical peaks at 289.8, 401.3, 531.6, and 708.3 eV assigned to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Fe 2p, respectively (Lee et al., 2017), which demonstrated all MFNPs had similar element composition. To study their fine structure, we characterized XPS spectra of Fe 2p, N 1 s, C 1 s, and O 1 s of B-MFNPs-3 and the deconvoluted peaks were fitted using the Gaussian fitting approach (Fig. 3b–e). Fig. 3b exhibited two Fe 2p peaks at 708.9 (Fe 2p1/2) and 708.3 (Fe 2p3/2) eV, which signify the existence of the Fe3O4 species (Zengin Kurt et al., 2017). Five peaks were determined in C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3c), which can be attributed to C elemental in C––C/C––H (282.9 eV), C–−OH (284.5 eV), CO or COOR (286.1 eV) and O––C––O (287.1 eV), respectively. Two peaks were observed in O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3d), which can be attributed to Fe3O4 (529.9 eV), and C–−OH, CO or COOR, O––C––O (530.9 eV), respectively (Li et al., 2016). Two peaks are observed in N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3e), which can be attributed to C––NH2 (397.4 eV) from CS and residual N element in CDs samples from urea (398.5 eV). Therefore, these results suggest that the CDs, CS and CMC was significantly incorporated in the surface of Fe3O4, which results in enhanced immobilization ability and dual responsiveness of the NPs. The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CCF, A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 were shown in Fig. 3f. In comparison, we also measured FT-IR spectra of the pristine CDs, CS and CMC samples. As seen in the spectrum of CDs, two obvious absorption peaks at 1360 and 1690 cm−1 can be observed, which can be owing to the symmetrical and asymmetric vibrational peak of –−COOH, indicating that the surface of CDs contains hydroxyl groups. In the CCF spectrum, the peak at 1556 cm−1 can be assigned to ––NH2 deformation vibration, which shifts to long wavelengths compared to that of the pristine CS because of the chemical bond between CS and Fe3O4 NPs. The FTIR spectra of A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 were almost identical, which demonstrated that they have a similar composition containing carbohydrates, magnetic materials and CDs.

Moreover, XRD analysis was carried out to investigate the crystallinity nature of the MFNPs, the patterns of CDs, CCF, A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 were exhibited in Fig. 4 a. The CS pattern has a diffraction peak center at 2 θ = 20.6°, which illustrates that CS powder is provided by Form II crystals. The diffraction peaks in CDs at 2 θ = 28.0° can be attributed to the amorphous carbon structure. The diffraction peaks appeared in CCF at 2 θ = 30.11°, 35.41°, 43.11°, 53.41°, 57.01°, and 62.61° is assigned to the crystal planes of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) of magnetite Fe3O4 (JCPDS11-0614), respectively. All MFNPs exhibited the characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe3O4, CS and CDs. Furthermore, the Fe3O4 maintain its initial crystalline phase after self-assembly process, which indicates that C, N or O atoms from CS, CMC and CDs are coated on the Fe3O4 NPs surface but not strongly incorporated into the Fe3O4 crystal lattice. Fig. 4b showed the DLS size distribution of CDs, CCF, A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 samples, which corresponding average particle sizes are 13.2 16.6, 22.7, 25.7, and 28.8 nm, respectively. These results are larger than TEM results because they are hydraulic radius (Chiang et al., 2018). As seen, the average particle size of MFNPs increases with the increase in a number of self-assembled layers and maintains a narrow particle size distribution. This can be partly attributed to the strong mixing, dispersion and evenly distributing of NPs obtained from IS-RPB. The B-MFNPs-3 sample has a larger hydraulic radius than A-MFNPs-3 sample due to the CDs exposed on the surface have a strong hydrophilicity. Thus, the HGT-LbL approaches are suitable for the preparation of monodisperse NPs. The changes of zeta potentials in the surface of the MFNPs during self-assembly were visualized in Fig. 4c. The zeta potentials exhibited positive and negative alternating trends versus self-assembly layer numbers. Because of the ionization of amino groups on CS side chain, the surface of CCF shows a positive zeta potential of 19.34 mV and is provided with the potential to adsorb the anionic material like CMC and CDs (Fig. 1d). On the contrary, when the outermost layer of MFNPs is CMC, their negative-charged surface can attract cationic polyelectrolytes. Thus, an LbL self-assembly method can be achieved to fabricate the MFNPs with muti-shells. The interaction mechanism between template protein BSA and MFNPs should be investigated to provide us how to improve the carrier design. In this work, the immobilization efficiency is mainly affected by the magnitude of electrostatic attraction between BSA and MFNPs and the degree of agglomeration of NPs. As seen in the Fig. 4d, the differences of zeta potential between B-MFNPs-5 and B-MFNPs-5-BSA reflect the adsorption capacity of BSA. At the high or low pH range, the zeta potential displays high values owing to the hydrodynamic force provided by shaking should be strong enough to destruct the weak hydrogen bonding. Moreover, the sorption of protein adjusts the interfacial characteristics of the MFNPs. As a result, the isoelectric point (pHPZC) of B-MFNPs-5-BSA is about 5.8, which is slightly higher than that of BSA (pHPZC 4.7). Therefore, by moderately changing the pH we can obtain a stable suspension in the medium and adjust the adsorption ability of target proteins.

To further study the pore structure of the as-obtained MFNPs, we performed the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. All curves elaborated in Fig. 4e can be classified as type-IV isotherm loops (Li et al., 2016). As calculated, the CCF samples have a BET specific surface area of 64.58 m−2 g-1, a BJH desorption cumulative pore volume of 0.13 cm-3 g-1 and an average pore size of 9.52 nm. These characteristics increase slightly after the incorporation of the biomass-derived materials and gradually raised with the increase in the number of surface assembly layers (Table S2), demonstrating that these materials can effectively improve their surface features. These surface structures would provide ideal physical space and nanosized channels for protein immobilization; thus, they are potentially favorable for protein immobilization (Yu et al., 2017).

Fig. 4f displayed TGA curves of different MFNPs in a nitrogen atmosphere. All samples have a slight weight loss near 100 °C, which can be attributed to the loss of water (Zengin Kurt et al., 2017). The weight loss of CCF mainly occurs at 203.2∼600.0 °C with approximately 15.51%, which can be assigned to the thermal decomposition of CS and CDs (Simões, Leitão, & da Silva, 2016). Similarly, the thermal decomposition stages of A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-2, B-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-4, and B-MFNPs-5 can be roughly divided into two stages: the first stage near 100 °C can correspond to the loss of water; the second stage at 203.3∼600.9 °C has a weight loss of about 36.71, 30.13, 40.92, 44.93, and 56.57%, respectively, which can be assigned to the decomposition of natural polymer and CDs. These results indicate the weight loss rate increase with the self-assembled layer numbers (Table S3), which further confirmed the successful LbL self-assembly in the IS-RPB. Fig. 5 a visualized the magnetic properties of the MFNPs. As seen, all MFNPs have approximately reversible hysteresis behavior and zero coercivity, which indicates they are provided with a typical superparamagnetic behavior. Furthermore, the saturation magnetization of the as-obtained CCF, A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 were 68.1, 51.6, 54.6 and 27.3 emu g−1, respectively, illuminating that saturation magnetization decline with the increase in layer numbers, which is can be explained by the incorporation of non-magnetic CMC, CS and CDs. As reported, NPs possessing saturation magnetizations above 16.3 emu g−1 could be magnetically separated from suspensions using a magnet. Thus, all MFNPs can be easily segregated from suspensions, and can possibly be magnetically drivable (Fig. 1c).

The PL spectra of CDs, CCF, A-MFNPs-3, B-MFNPs-3 and B-MFNPs-5 under different excitation wavelengths were tested here to evaluate their fluorescence properties (Fig. S4). The differences in the maximum emission wavelength versus different excitation wavelength were depicted in Fig. 5b. All MFNPs obtained via the HGT-LbL method maintain the wavelength-dependent natures of cellulose-based CDs (Shen et al., 2016). Moreover, three new phenomena can be observed. (i) the fluorescence emission intensity of CCF is relatively weaker because of only a small amount of CDs is wrapped in a short micro-mixing time. (ii) The B-MFNPs-3 has stronger fluorescence emission than that of A-MFNPs-3 with same layer number owing to CDs can anchor on the CS via an electrostatic coupling, thereby the agglomeration of CDs and fluorescence quenching are suppressed. However, in the preparation process of A-MFNPs-3, the dissociative CDs and CMC will be a competitor for layer-by-layer self-assembly in the mixture, thus the amount of attached CDs was restricted partly. (iii) The fluorescence intensity increase with the rise in the number of layers. Thus, suitable layer-by-layer self-assembly method and layer numbers can provide an effective strategy for controlling and improving the fluorescence intensity and preventing fluorescence quenching (Fig. 1c). The in vitro cytotoxicity of B-MFNPs-5 was determined via conducting MTT assays against HeLa (cancer) and HeLung (normal) cells. As depicted in Fig. 5c, B-MFNPs-5 NPs have no instinct negative influence on the viability of the cells after incubation with various concentrations suspension (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μg mL−1) for 48 h. Therefore, the polyelectrolyte multilayers are substantially free of cytotoxicity because all raw materials have high biocompatibility, which is advantageous for the potential biological and biomedicine applications.

For the aim to investigate of the rate-limiting step in the immobilization mechanism, pseudo-first-order (Eq. (1)), pseudo-second-order (Eq. (2)), and intraparticle-diffusion (Eq. (3)) kinetic models were used to describe the kinetic data.
(1)
Q t
=
Q e
( 1 - e x p (
- k
1
) )
(2)
Q t
=
k 2
Q e 2
t
1 +
k 2
Q e
t
(3)
Q t
=
k i
t
1 / 2
+ C
Where Q e (mg g−1) and Q t (mg g−1) are the adsorption capacities at equilibrium and the adsorption amount at time t (min), respectively. k 1 (min−1), k 2 (mg g−1 min−1) and k i (mg g−1 min−1) are pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intraparticle-diffusion rate constants of adsorption, respectively. C is the intraparticle-diffusion adsorption constant.

Pseudo-first-order model assumes the rate of occupation of the immobilization points to be proportional to the number of unoccupied sites, pseudo-second-order model describes the chemisorption occurring through the exchange or sharing or of electrons between the supports and template protein, whereas intraparticle-diffusion model assumes that no external mass-transfer resistance occurs in the immobilization process. Fig. 6b–d showed the linear fitting results of different kinetic models, which kinetic parameters were displayed in Table 2 . We found that the pseudo-second-order model fitted the experimental data better than pseudo-first-order model because of their linear regression correlation coefficient (R 2) were higher than 0.99, demonstrating that the rate-controlling step might be ascribed to chemisorption. Furthermore, the k 2 and Q e values of the MFNPs are in order of CCF < A-MFNPs-3 < B-MFNPs-3 < B-MFNPs-5. This indicates that the interactions between MFNPs and BSA increased with the biomass layer number, probably due to the improvement of surface pore structure and the increase in active functional groups. Moreover, the immobilization capacity of B-MFNPs-3 is higher than that of A-MFNPs-3, because of the stable formation of CS-CDs complex in the surface contribute to enhancing the surface activity. In general, there are mainly two stages in immobilization process: (i) the template protein migrates from liquid phase to the liquid-solid interface (called external mass-transfer), and (ii) the enriched template protein diffuses to internal pores (called internal intraparticle-diffusion). Ideally, if the immobilization is only decided to internal mass-transfer resistance, the adsorption capacity should be controlled by time. As seen in intraparticle-diffusion plots for the BSA immobilization on different MFNPs (Fig. 6d), more than one linear region exist in the whole plots, which demonstrates that the immobilization is controlled by two or more stages. Furthermore, the rate constants in each step follow the order of ki1 > ki2 > ki3 (Table 2). Three stages can be divided in the immobilization: the instant diffusion stage might be described as the first region with abrupt slope, in which substantial BSA molecule from bulk phase was migrated fast to the surface of MFNPs owing to the attracting of surface functional groups. After nearly all the exterior active sites were occupied, the BSA transfers into the pores of the supports, and then were immobilized by the interior surface of pores, which referred to the second stage. In the third step, the intraparticle-diffusion rate constants continued to decrease, demonstrating the equilibrium state has been nearly reached at last. Moreover, the rate constants ki1 and ki2 for adsorption of BSA on B-MFNPs-5 are much higher than that of CCF, which might be owing to more active points on the surface or inside the pores of B-MFNPs-5 for attracting BSA and improving the diffusion rate. In summary of the kinetic investigations, it is implied that both the intraparticle-diffusion and the chemical chelating reaction between BSA and active sites of the MFNPs had a crucial influence on the immobilization processes.

The effect of the initial concentration of BSA on the immobilization capacity of MFNPs at three different temperatures (25, 35, and 45 °C) is displayed in Fig. 7 a–d. As shown, a rapid increase of immobilization capacity of MFNPs with increasing BSA concentration from 0.1 to 0.6 mg mL−1 was observed, and a saturation status is reached over 0.8 mg mL−1. The BSA immobilization capacity was increased as the temperature increased, indicating that higher temperatures facilitate the BSA immobilization onto the MFNPs. The immobilization amounts of BSA adsorbed onto B-MFNPs-5 are much higher than CCF, demonstrating the specific immobilization points complementary to the model protein with functional groups, spatial structure and molecular size appear in the biomass layer. To investigate the template affinity and immobilization behaviors of MFNPs, Freundlich (Eq. (4)) and Langmuir (Eq. (5)) isotherm are adopted to describe the immobilization isotherms.
(4)
Q e
=
k F
C e
1 n
(5)
Q e
=
Q m
k L
C e
1 +
k L
C e
Where C e is the equilibrium concentration of the BSA, k F and n is the Freundlich equilibrium constant, Q m is the maximum immobilization capacity, and k L is the Langmuir equilibrium constant. The parameters of the isotherm models were calculated by linear regression, which results are shown in Figs. S5-6 and summarized in Table 3 . As seen in Table 3, Langmuir linear fitting yields better results for all MFNPs (with the regression coefficient higher than 0.99) than the Freundlich equation, demonstrating a monolayer immobilization behavior. Moreover, the calculated k m and Q m value of B-MFNPs-5 is significantly larger compared with CCF, further indicating the improved thickness of biomass is favorable for BSA immobilization. Moreover, the thermodynamic parameters such as Gibbs free energy change (ΔG), enthalpy change (ΔH) and entropy change (ΔS) for BSA immobilization using MFNPs-supports were evaluated and the results were exhibited in Table 3 (the calculation process given in the Supplementary File). The negative ΔG values indicate the feasibility and spontaneity of BSA immobilization by the MFNPs, which are improved with the increase of layer numbers. The positive value of ΔH proved the endothermic nature of the BSA immobilization onto the MFNPs. The positive value of ΔS confirmed the increased randomness of the solid-liquid interface during the immobilization process.

Regeneration of the carriers is an important step for reversible protein immobilization techniques and a key factor in enhancing process economy (Ren, Abbood, He, Peng, & Huang, 2013). Thus, it is necessary to evaluate the regeneration efficiency of the supports. To demonstrate the reusability of the biomass modified magnetic NPs, the adsorption-desorption cycle was repeated five times using the same MFNPs in BSA solution (PBS buffer, pH 8.0, 10 mM) with a feed concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1. After immobilization, the MFNPs were washed repeatedly with 0.1 M NaCl solution to remove the embedded and loosely adhered protein. Fig. S8 exhibited that B-MFNPs-5 lose only 3.5% of its immobilization capacity and 5% of its PL intensity even after five times cycles, indicating that the MFNPs can be reused frequently without almost significant loss in immobilization and fluorescent performance and can be utilized extensively in industrial activities.

In conclusion, we report a facile high-gravity-technology-assisted approach for the synthesis of MFNPs. The CS/CDs@Fe3O4 NPs with small particle size and uniform distribution were in situ synthesized in IS-RPB successfully. After LbL self-assembling of different biomass-based polyelectrolyte in the IS-RPB, the obtained multilayered MFNPs not only maintained superparamagnetic properties, but also showed superior surface properties and pore structure, and improved fluorescent emission. Their immobilization results indicate the MFNPs with a stable structure and active surface exhibited a relatively high protein immobilization capacity; the maximum immobilization capacity can reach 59.12 mg/g. We also found the monolayer adsorption is the main immobilization mode in these systems. The chemical chelating reaction between BSA and the active site of MFNPs and intraparticle-diffusion had an important influence on the immobilization processes. These bifunctional MFNPs are expected to be used as a smart immobilization system via the magnetic drive and fluorescence tracking, which also have promising applications in various biomedical fields such as the magnetic fluid hyperthermia treatment, magnetic resonance imaging and relative research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Graduate Technological Innovation Project of Beijing Institute of Technology (BIT) (grant number 2017CX10023). We thank the Instrumental Analysis Center of Beijing Institute of Technology and Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2018.08.088.
